name: Google
shortModel: Makes most of its money showing ads — mostly on Google Search and YouTube.
surprise: Google ads bring in over $200 billion a year. The 'free' search isn't free — advertisers pay every time someone clicks.

name: Meta (Instagram & Facebook)
shortModel: Nearly all of its money comes from ads in Instagram and Facebook feeds.
surprise: About 98% of Meta's revenue is advertising. You are not the customer — advertisers are.

name: Apple
shortModel: Sells iPhones — and takes a cut of every app and subscription sold on those phones.
surprise: Apple's Services (App Store, iCloud, Apple Music) make more profit than the Mac and iPad combined.

name: Microsoft
shortModel: Sells monthly subscriptions to Office/365 and rents out cloud computing power through Azure.
surprise: Microsoft barely sells 'boxed software' anymore — almost everything is a subscription.

name: Amazon
shortModel: Online store sales are the biggest revenue, but its cloud business (AWS) earns most of the profit.
surprise: AWS rents servers to companies like Netflix, Airbnb, and the U.S. government — it makes way more profit than shipping packages.

name: Netflix
shortModel: Charges a monthly subscription to stream shows and movies.
surprise: Netflix spends about $17 billion a year on making and licensing content. That's their #1 cost.

name: Tesla
shortModel: Sells electric cars — mostly the Model Y and Model 3.
surprise: Tesla also sells 'regulatory credits' to other carmakers who can't meet emissions rules. That's pure profit.

name: Nvidia
shortModel: Sells the chips that power AI and video games. Data centers are now their biggest customer.
surprise: Nvidia chips used to be mostly for gaming. Now AI companies buy so many chips, gaming is a smaller slice of the business.

name: Visa
shortModel: Takes a tiny percentage of every credit/debit card swipe — billions of times a day.
surprise: Visa doesn't lend money or issue cards. Banks do that. Visa just runs the network — and earns a fee on every transaction.

name: Mastercard
shortModel: Takes a tiny percentage of every card transaction it processes — just like Visa.
surprise: Visa and Mastercard together handle most of the world's card payments. They don't lend money — they just run the rails.

name: Coca-Cola
shortModel: Sells concentrated syrup to bottling companies — Coca-Cola the company doesn't actually deliver soda to stores.
surprise: The famous red truck? That's a bottler, not Coke itself. Coca-Cola the company is mainly a giant syrup supplier.

name: McDonald's
shortModel: Most McDonald's are owned by franchisees — McDonald's collects rent and royalties from them.
surprise: McDonald's is basically a giant real-estate company. They own the land and buildings and rent them to franchisees.

name: Starbucks
shortModel: Sells coffee directly at thousands of company-owned stores around the world.
surprise: Unlike McDonald's, Starbucks owns and runs most of its stores itself — that's a very different business model.

name: Chipotle
shortModel: Sells burritos and bowls at company-owned restaurants — Chipotle doesn't franchise.
surprise: Every single Chipotle in the world is owned by Chipotle the company. No franchisees.

name: Domino's Pizza
shortModel: Most stores are franchised — Domino's the company makes money from royalties plus selling dough and supplies to franchisees.
surprise: Domino's also runs a huge dough-making and supply business, selling ingredients to its own franchisees.

name: DoorDash
shortModel: Takes a cut (around 25-30%) of every food delivery order it processes for restaurants.
surprise: Restaurants pay DoorDash a big chunk of every order. That's why delivery is so much more expensive than dine-in.

name: Uber
shortModel: Takes a cut of every ride and every Uber Eats food delivery — it doesn't own the cars.
surprise: Uber doesn't own a single car. Drivers own them. Uber just runs the app that connects riders to drivers.

name: Airbnb
shortModel: Takes a service fee on every booking — Airbnb doesn't own any of the houses.
surprise: Airbnb owns zero properties. They just run the marketplace and take a cut from both the host and the guest.

name: Spotify
shortModel: Monthly subscriptions for ad-free music — plus ads played to free-tier listeners.
surprise: Spotify pays about 70 cents of every dollar to music labels and artists. That's why they keep raising prices.

name: Roblox
shortModel: Roblox takes a cut every time someone buys Robux to spend in games other kids made.
surprise: Roblox itself doesn't make most of the games. Kid developers do — and Roblox keeps a big slice of every purchase.

name: EA Sports
shortModel: Sells sports games like Madden, FIFA (EA Sports FC), and NHL — plus in-game purchases for Ultimate Team.
surprise: EA makes more money from Ultimate Team card packs than from selling the games themselves.

name: Take-Two Interactive
shortModel: Makes blockbuster games like Grand Theft Auto and NBA 2K — plus in-game purchases.
surprise: GTA V (released in 2013) is still one of the highest-earning entertainment products ever made — over $8 billion.

name: Nintendo
shortModel: Sells the Switch console and first-party games like Mario, Zelda, and Pokémon.
surprise: Nintendo barely makes money on hardware. The Switch's profit really comes from the games — Mario Kart 8 has sold over 60 million copies.

name: Disney
shortModel: Theme parks, cruises, and resort tickets are now Disney's largest profit source — bigger than movies.
surprise: When a hit Disney movie comes out, it really pays off years later: as a parks ride, merch, and a Disney+ show.

name: Nike
shortModel: Designs and sells athletic shoes and clothing — through its own stores, its app, and retail partners.
surprise: Nike doesn't run the factories that make its shoes. Other companies do, and Nike pays them. Nike focuses on design and brand.

name: Costco
shortModel: Most of Costco's profit comes from membership fees — not from the markup on items it sells.
surprise: Costco sells stuff at almost zero markup. The annual membership fee is the real moneymaker.

name: Walmart
shortModel: Sells groceries and general merchandise at thin margins — but at enormous scale (the U.S.'s biggest grocer).
surprise: Walmart is the largest private employer in the U.S. — about 1.6 million American workers.

name: Shopify
shortModel: Monthly subscriptions to small businesses to run their online stores — plus a cut of payments processed through Shopify.
surprise: Lots of brands you see on Instagram and TikTok ads are actually running on Shopify behind the scenes.

name: PayPal
shortModel: Takes a small fee on each payment processed through PayPal or Venmo.
surprise: PayPal owns Venmo. The 'free' Venmo payments to friends are free — but PayPal makes money from businesses that accept Venmo.

name: Robinhood
shortModel: Doesn't charge trade commissions — earns money by routing your trade orders to big trading firms and on interest from your cash.
surprise: Robinhood trades feel 'free' but the app is paid by Wall Street firms in exchange for routing your orders to them.

name: Coinbase
shortModel: Charges a fee every time someone buys or sells crypto on its platform.
surprise: When crypto prices crash, Coinbase makes a lot less money — because fewer people trade. The business goes up and down with crypto hype.

name: Adobe
shortModel: Monthly subscriptions to creative software like Photoshop, Illustrator, and Acrobat (PDFs).
surprise: Adobe stopped selling Photoshop as a one-time purchase years ago. Now everyone has to pay every month, forever.

name: Salesforce
shortModel: Sells subscriptions to giant business software that tracks customers (CRM = Customer Relationship Management).
surprise: Almost every Fortune 500 company uses Salesforce. That's a huge moat — companies hate switching once it's installed.

name: JPMorgan Chase
shortModel: Makes money lending out customers' deposits at higher rates than it pays them — plus investment banking fees.
surprise: Every dollar deposited in a Chase account isn't sitting in a vault. The bank lends most of it out to other people.

name: Goldman Sachs
shortModel: Investment banking — helping huge companies buy each other, go public (IPO), and trade securities.
surprise: Goldman doesn't take regular checking accounts like Chase does. It mainly serves big companies and rich people.

name: Procter & Gamble
shortModel: Owns dozens of household brands (Tide, Pampers, Gillette, Crest) and sells them to retailers like Walmart and Target.
surprise: You probably use 5+ P&G products before lunch and don't even know it — Tide, Crest, Pampers, Bounty, Gillette are all theirs.

name: Live Nation
shortModel: Concert tickets (it owns Ticketmaster) plus sponsorships and concession sales at venues.
surprise: Live Nation owns Ticketmaster — that's why those service fees on concert tickets feel so big.
